speaker: interviewer
text: September 29, 1995. Survivor is Ruth  Krol, nee Gerstel. Interviewer is Vera Goldberg. Out of Sydney, Australia. The language is English. And this is tape one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today we are having an interview with Ruth Krol. The date is the 29th of September, 1995. My name is Vera Goldberg. And we are in Sydney, Australia. Can you tell me your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Ruth Krol.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ruth, what was your name when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had an additional name-- Ruth Edith Gerstel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your family name was--

speaker: interviewee
text: My family name was Gerstel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: Funny nicknames-- Puty. I don't know. Because my hair was so curly, so they called me Puty.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me what is your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 14th, first, 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born, Ruth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Katowice, now Poland. At the time I was born, it was Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you grew up in a German city, a German-speaking city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Actually, it was-- both languages were used.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: German/Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you learn Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: At school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you live in Katowice for all your growing up time?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Until I was deported.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were a very loving family. My parents. And I only had one brother. That means four of us. And as my parents were business people, an aunt of mine lived with us and looked after us children instead of my mom. But we had a nanny as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, what was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Fritz. It-- Fritz. In Polish, Frederic is what they called him. But Fritz was his first name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of work did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a businessman. We had a-- a tobacconist. And then government stamps and different papers, you know, lease papers and-- and things like that. And we had also, later, a government lottery.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father had, like, an agency for different official--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --types of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: --it was from the state. You can say an agency.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned that your mother worked, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They worked, both, in the same shop-- in the same business.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you live close to the business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It's the same place. It a five-story place. We lived on the second floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the business was in the-- that same building?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the  same building, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What? The ground floor?

speaker: interviewee
text: The business was, yes. So we're facing the street. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in the heart of the city. It was a very well-kept business and a very prosperous business.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was in the actual downtown center of Katowice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was the center of the town. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grety, or Margaret. Grety is shortening for Margaret.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she called Greta? Was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of a lady was your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, very-- a-- a good business  lady. A fantastic mother. Very good wife. I only can say a real good thing. Could be very energetic. I remember once, as a very small school child, I told her a lie, and she didn't hesitate to punish me for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: So right away, what I never could say to my father, I never remember that he would have touched me. But for this lie, I was well-punished by my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she-- she kept things very much under control. She--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about your brother. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's name was Norbert. He was-- we were four-years difference in age. He was a highly intelligent boy. I suppose he inherited it from my father. Unfortunately, he could only go to, I think, 14 years of age to school because the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Norbert younger than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Four-- four years younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned that you had an aunt living with you in the house as well. What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Else.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's sister was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's sister. She was an unmarried person, and replaced, in every respect, my mother. Was very good to us, was what a mother should do-- bring the children to school, you know, and look also after the well-being of the house, see that the cook does the right thing and the cleaner does the right thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was a fairly good person. And so she-- she really ran the household while your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was in business.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was a business lady.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have anybody else living in the house as well besides Else?

speaker: interviewee
text: In later years, another lived with us. But it was-- she actually lost her husband, so that my father said, come live with us. It really doesn't matter one person more. And she had a little place of her own in the same township, but my father wanted that she has her meals and everything with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you actually had an extended family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Family. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the second auntie, what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hannah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was she relate-- was she your father's sister, also?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's sister. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's grandparents-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my grandpa's name was Natan. And he was very popular. He worked, you know, not for himself only, but practically for the whole township. He was a volunteer firefighter, besides his business. And absolute popular. He, unfortunately, passed away very young, was 54. And it was like-- like they have here, a state burial. So it looked with him, not very Jewish, because music was on, you know. The typical German way. I heard was played. And it-- it I still remember him, because he was a good grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your-- and your grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my grandmother conducted then the business they had. Continued to conduct it together with my aunts. There were quite a few aunts. But in time, they all got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your other grandparents? Your mother's parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Father's.

speaker: interviewer
text: Father's parents. sorry. Father's.

speaker: interviewee
text: I described mother's and father's parents. They-- I loved them very much, because mother's parents were the rich grandparents whereas his grandparents were restricted. And my father even helped out. But my heart was more with them. Maybe out of [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also, I have the looks of my grandfather. So you resemble him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was very light blonde with blue eyes, and I got that from him-- had that from him. And he was a-- a religious man. He was a cantor in the Myslowice synagogue, which is a little township by Katowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they live in-- in the little town? In Myslowice?

speaker: interviewee
text: They lived-- actually, they had rooms in the synagogue. And a huge garden. And I remember how I-- when I came over, the hammock was between trees. And I felt in heavens. They were very, very good to me, because I was their only granddaughter. So they had-- my brother came later. And grandpa only had us two.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a lot of fun when you visited them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very. Very. They were very close to my heart. And also, in-- after I matriculated, I went in the same township to a German-- it was called haushaltungsschule. That meant-- Saturday, my parents were religious. I wasn't allowed to travel, so I stayed overnight with my grandparents, which was big fun for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you really enjoyed that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. I had-- I loved them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me how religious were your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father was deeply religious. But not religious so far that he ran with the [INAUDIBLE]. But I remember every morning he laid his tefillin. And was strictly kosher with our household. And all the holidays, of course, were celebrated. But-- like, in my township in Katowice, when there were Jewish holidays, the High Holidays I'm speaking, the town was dead. All shops were closed, and there wasn't any life, because most shop owners were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how much of the population was Jewish in Katowice?

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: Half of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a really very, very Jewish town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very Jewish town. Approximately of 100,000. And Jewish people kept very much together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in a Jewish neighborhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was the center.

speaker: interviewer
text: --because it was the center of the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was mixed?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was mixed. Also, the place I lived in was mixed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had neighbors in your building who were Jewish and not Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did people get on? What were relationships before the war between the Jewish and the non-Jewish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, before the war, say, it-- it changed all since Hitler was on. And that was already before the war. This relationship with German people changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did it change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Say, '36. I remember even in school, I remember, that the girls being on holidays-- mostly after holidays when they came back from Germany-- we could feel-- we were five Jewish girls in my class-- so we could feel this strangeness. You know, they-- they were quite different.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was this coolness?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: This different feeling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Entirely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was already in 1936?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Because it was a border town, and it was very German, especially through that-- that I belonged to this language, to this language group. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I felt it very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about this change in the relationship with the girls in your class?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we just had to carry on, because there was only one German high school. And we-- my Polish wasn't good enough to go to a Polish high school, and I never had the intention to sort of be closer to Poles, because they [INAUDIBLE] anti-Semitic without Hitler. So they were anti-Semitic, so that was one thing which was very far from me. So so long I could, I went to all German schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this change in the attitude of your classmates come as a shock to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. Because, you know, they-- they were in my place. When-- when I got teased, they got teased. It was before, very friendly. And then sort of they started to hold a distance.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you  no longer invited to their houses or to their birthday parties?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that stopped. That stopped. I-- we were very close, the five Jewish girls. We stick together like relatives. We-- but the closeness with the other girls wasn't anymore there since the Hitler time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And before Hitler times--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were very friendly. We were all like one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So up until 1936, you really experienced no feeling of being different?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Not at all. Just on the contrary. I played in the German team, you know, when-- when sport was on and so on. But that stopped. Say, even my feelings stopped. I didn't go anymore to-- to German sports. I went then to Bar Kochba, which was a Jewish sport ground. And, well, they made me feel what I  am.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it is a terrible feeling when you were born, and you thought you are German. And overnight, you know you're not German. They made me feel that I don't belong to them and that I am Jewish. And they brought the Jewishness very much out of me. Before, I-- I took it as a religion. But then I started to feel, well, it is more. I started to feel, we must be a nation as well. It's not only a religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk a little bit actually about school. Where did you go to primary school?

speaker: interviewee
text: To primary school, it was called mittelschule. I went in my hometown, same. In Katowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after--

speaker: interviewee
text: And four-- that was four years. And then we made an examination for-- I went to the Deutsche [INAUDIBLE] Gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was a--

speaker: interviewee
text: So that-- that was then the continuation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you finished gymnasium?

speaker: interviewee
text: I finished my gymnasium in-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't know now. What it would have been? In '37, I think. '37, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do when you finished gymnasium?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went for a year to a-- a finishing school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what did you learn in the finishing school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything what is necessary to be a fantastic housewife. I learned cooking. I learned sewing. I learned shopping, because even that you got to know. And even-- even cleaning and all what you need to-- to have a nice home and to be good in your house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was the-- the finishing school also in Katowice?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you stayed with your grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Myslowice, where my grandparents lived. And by train, it was not so long. It was 20 minutes by train. So only Friday night, I didn't go home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you stayed over?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed till Sunday by my grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your-- your life as you were growing up. Did you have holidays? Did you travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I-- I-- from small on, I said, the first travel I remember was maybe I was five years of age with my mom and grandma to [INAUDIBLE]. That was in Czechoslovakia. And from then on, we-- when I went to school, we had always holidays. We went to-- mostly to Germany to a seaside resort, because we lived close to mountains, but not to the sea. So for, say, six weeks in my holidays, we went to mostly Kolobrzeg, which is on the Baltic Sea. And when there was a bit of time left, we went for two weeks to the mountains to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which mountains were those in Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they must have been the Beskydy. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what sort of holiday activities, besides going to the seaside, did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, different sports. I played tennis, and I swan. I learned swimming in Kolobrzeg as a very young kid. And, well, when we got a bit older, we learned dancing, too. And I-- I went a lot to the theater and to concerts. And every weekend, we went to the mountains, to the Polish Beskydy, and had climbing and so on. That was what we liked-- camping, climbing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you do this with? Your family or your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I had a very good girlfriend, and with her I mostly did the climbing part. I had other girlfriends who didn't like the mountains, because it was too too strenuous. So, but this one girlfriend of mine, we did everything together. her dad had a Fiat, a little Fiat. So we just were bundled in and went to Szczyrk. That was a resort. Or Ostrow. And from there, it was very easy to climb mountains and have a good time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about in winter? What kind of winter sports did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did skiing. But I-- I was a bit clumsy. I wasn't the best skier, I must admit. I was too-- I don't know why, but I wasn't good in skiing. I did that because it was good exercise, but nothing like the others could jump and things like that, which I never even tried. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of a-- a girl were you? What sort of a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a fairly quiet child. I had deep respect for my father. He was very strict. And, you know, not like I see today. They're more like friends, like they would be one age. That wasn't the case. I respected my father deeply. And I loved my mother deeply. That is-- I think that is the right expression.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what sort of things did you do in town besides school and-- and your-- did you have any particular hobbies? Was there anything you particularly enjoyed doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sports. Sports was my big hobby. And then we had also, for the younger generation of the lodge, we had a thing called Jugenbund, where we went once weekly and there were speeches and different attractions.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say 'lodge,' what lodge are you talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: B'nai B'rith. B'nai B'rith loge in Katowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Jugenbund was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was for the younger generation, for the children of-- of the members.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents were members of B'nai B'rith?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Jugenbund had boys and girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I-- I was talking about that. I met my first boyfriends there.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the Jugenbund?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the Jugenbund. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this the sort of meeting place? This was where the social activity took place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And lots of, as you say, boys and girls, met and married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Through the Jugenbund?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was through-- yes. Through meetings of people and going out together and having excursions together. So that made us closer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you actually do this? On the weekend? This was a weekend activity?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was one day during the week, if I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What? In the evenings?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the evening, yes. And I was so-- today you would say 'backwards kept,' because I remember I was 16 years of age, and when we finished, I wasn't allowed to go  in my own home. My nanny came and picked me up, so that wouldn't exist today.

speaker: interviewer
text: But was that-- that wouldn't-- was the norm for other girls of your age at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some. Not all. Not all. My father was too strict, I think. Maybe today's time gives me the idea, but he was a real strict father. 7 o'clock, I had to be home. And heavens forbid it was later. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you look back on those years that you were a child, and as you were growing up as a teenager, what are your overall memories of those early years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very happy memories. I had things and did things not everybody could do because it was fairly expensive to travel or to go to the theater or to play tennis. And here it doesn't seem to be expensive, but at the time I did these things, it was quite expensive. And, actually, nothing was-- by my parents-- nothing was too expensive for me. There wasn't a thing I wouldn't have to make me happy. And it was-- I was quite privileged child, because not only that I had a nanny, but I remember I had a lady coming to my place. And she taught me languages. We had, in the high school, Latin. And that was a bit hard for girls. So I had the help at home for that as well. And I had somebody for English. So it wasn't too hard for me here when I arrived in Australia, because I could understand a bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you already had a big advantage in the way that you had grown up and been educated?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's stop there.
